chunks.text: Uh, good morning, Rahet. Morning. Me again. Yes, you again. No, don't worry, we had Dion three times. There we go. Today I'm only wearing my Gisborne District Council hat, and I would love to chat to you about, unitary authorities mostly, because- That'd be great. That would be it... we are a, a, a- Yep, that'd be great. We've got 15 minutes of your time.

Um, w- we've got your submission, thank you very much. Take it as read, but highlight a few points and leave time for questions. Yes. Fire away. Thank you. Awesome, and I've also got, Shane McGee and Jocelyne Allen here to support me. So today I will focus on four areas of particular importance for us here in Te Tairāwhiti that we ask you to consider, addressing some, through some changes.

So let's start first off, there are only a few unitary authorities a- across New Zealand, but you would be well aware that the government is looking at moving more to that model in, in some way. So we thought we would bring some stuff which obviously stands out for us. Unitary authorities, as you know, do both regional and local authority functions, both which will change substantially under this new system. This means that the impacts of these bills for Gisborne District Council and other unitary authorities are different to those that regional and local councils will

experience. This is not recognized in the bills as they are currently drafted. In fact, there are almost no specific mention of unitary authorities at all. A key example of how unitary authorities will be affected differently to others is in the preparation and also then the implementation of the new plans.

So let me use some examples with you. Unitary authorities will have to, having already prepared the regional spatial plan, prepare both a land use plan and an environmental plan at the same time, without any provision for additional time to achieve this. Preparing three separate parts of a combined plan will result in unnecessary duplication and inefficiency. So this duplication and inefficiency will then carry through to how we implement these plans. The bills require both a planning consent and a natural resources

permit, which in unitary regions will be issued by the same council. For us, this is a backwards step, as we currently only require one consent under one plan. So what we are asking is we seek explicit, explicit identification of unitary authorities in this bill, and provisions allowing us to avoid wasting time and resources, because that's ultimately why the government is changing the bill, and we are happy to discuss this, with officials in detail later, and it is addressed in our, submission as well. Then I take you to our second point

is the implementation timeframes. Um, it is the timeframes for the development of the first generation plans is not workable at all. We would be given six months from the release of national directions to prepare a full regional spatial plan, which is not doable when you look at the requirement for what this plan must include, and the importance of the plan for the future prosperi- prosperity of our region. In our submission, we have suggested 15 months from the release of the national direction would, would, would, would be more appropriate.

But I also know that some of our colleagues in other regions, such as Canterbury, have s- said maybe what about 24 months to prepare robust first generation spatial plans. Now the next, the nine-month timeframe for preparing land use as well as na- natural environment plans is unreasonable, particularly for unitary authorities like us who have to prepare both.

This should be extended for unitary authorities to recognize our dual workload to at least 18 months. Next, I would like to quickly touch on regulat- regulatory relief. Um, introducing regulat- relief in the current form, will not work and it, it's aligned with rate caps. The potential financial cost on re- of regulatory relief will affect council's ability to keep funding within the rates cap, while maintaining provision of vital infrastructure and services.

This will be particularly felt in regions and districts like us here in the T- Te Tairāwhiti, who has a very low rating base. Transitional provisions resulting in historic claims will exacerbate this and force our current community to pay for previous private land use choices.

We can also see the regulatory relief resulting in a proliferation of litigation through the Environment Court, which will just add to the cost required to be borne by our ratepayers. There's also a high probability that if regulatory relief is not removed from the bills, protection of significant sites will be unaffordable, and this will lead to further degradation of biodiversity and natural, historical, and cultural features across Te Tairāwhiti, and possibly the entire country. Despite these concerns, we acknowledge that

commitments have been made for regulatory relief to be included in this reform. Our submission is that if it is to stay, sensible changes need to be made, and we've made some, suggestions in our written submission. And then second to last, I would like to talk a little bit about sustainable land use transition. Um, you know what's been happening in the Te Tairāwhiti in the last few years, what happens when we see these, extreme weather events, and this is exacerbated by unsustainable land use of highly erosion-prone

land, and how this relates to the proposals in this bill. Our Sustainable Land Use Transition program is the blueprint we will use toMove our region forward. It has set out a bold but achievable vision to transition highly erosion, erosion-prone land to permanent resilient vegetative cover. We are investigate- investigating s- certified sustainable land use plans as a key transition delivery mechanism.

The Natural Environment Bill must make it explicit that integration of freshwater farm plans and property-specific erosion management sus- such as sustainable land use plans is permitted. Council has applied, applied for a plan stop exemption to progress our sustainable land use plan change to address land with high risk of erosion as we await this decision. It is vital that we can carry such provisions over to the new system to be enable this transition to carry on. Explicit provisions must be made in both bills for regions like us with extreme

natural, hazard risk to apply more stringent rules than national defaults, rather than leaving this to be addressed in national instruments. We have started on this transition journey, and we need to make sure that the new planning system provides the regulatory levers required to get that right. And then just a few final points. Um, te tiriti partnerships with Mana Whenua on protecting Maori rights and interests are very important to us here, and it's just not theoretical. We live and breathe them every day in our

region, with the highest proportion of Maori in the population of Aotearoa. I encourage the committee to consider the points in our submission and other submissions in relation to this very carefully. And then secondly, the overlapping timeframes of reform implementation we are responsible for, which is the resource management, emergency management, the purpose of l- local government, reorganization of local government, infrastructure funding, rates capping, all- Mm... place a huge burden on our council and the whole

sector. We need resources to support this, otherwise it will fall on our ratepayers. And in a world of restricted rates increases, it will reduce what we have available to spend on essential services. Thank you very much for your time. And I have Joslin and Shane to answer all the difficult questions.

Yes, and I'm, I'm just curious if... You're operating in a different time there. I think the clock says five past eight, but never mind. Um- I'm in Wellington- So the time stops in Wellington. Anyway, our first question comes from Ryan Hamilton. Thanks, Chair, and thanks for your submission. I just wanted to pick up on one point.

Uh, obviously you made quite a broad, broad submission, but particularly around your comments r- in, in your written submission around data system monitoring and environmental data systems. Do you have... I mean, are you using any existing systems that you think could be replicated, or are you seeing inconsistencies across, you know, your, you know, your areas? How could, how could government better enhance and support that space and/or through this bill?

Shane. Uh, we have some specific data. What we would need to do would be to get our technical people, and as has been said by a number of other submitters, we're happy to provide that, that information. Uh, most councils have quite significant data sets, and that, takes a lot of resources to get those, and actually ensuring that we can have those, applied appropriately would be what we're asking.

Yeah, and one of the comments obviously from our environmental commissioner, you know, it's, it's, it's one of the biggest, I guess, pain points he sees, or opportunities. Are, are you seeing inconsistency across the way councils report on that data or, you know, do we need more standardization across...? Standardization is always a good thing.

Uh, being aware of what is required and that, reduces costs, makes things, as, as is intended by the changes to the whole regulation, trying to get things standardized across the country is a benefit. Yeah. Okay. Thank you. Yep. Uh, Lan Pham. Kia ora. Thank you so much for your submission, and apologies that you have to squeeze such, like, serious implications into such a short time. Um, so I just wanna pick up on a few things. So you say the life supporting capacity goal is essentially too low of a bar, and you're looking for

ecological integrity and the same with biodiversity. Instead of no net gain, you're looking for no net loss. But you also talk about- No net... a significant emission, um... Sorry, omission, not emission, being no explicit reference to the integrated management of natural resources. Can, can you describe in what way you would like this recognized in the bill?

Joslin. Um, thank you. Yes, and thanks for picking up on some of the, more poignant parts of our submission. Um, integration, the way that, that we see it and, and certainly how we felt it was adopted, and recognized through the men- through the Resource Management Act, s- became part of that, section 5 in part two, the sort of engine room in which we were able to operate, through our planning frameworks and, and ultimately the, the decisions we made.

So, we very much see, and certainly as a unitary authority, the importance of, of integration across our, our asset management, our planning, over to the, protection of our, our, protected taonga. We see it as vital that, the integrated management, be uplifted, to the extent possible.

Uh, Rachel Thank you. Two questions. Uh, one is, you were, and apologies I don't have the... I'm having some tech problems, I don't have your submission in front of me. But one, issue was you said the, sediment plans for highly erosion, erosion-prone areas should be permitted. If you could just tell us a bit more about that.

And then we also had Ngāti Porou in before the hapū saying, that d- the, part of the country that you're in has its own, special issues, you know, with erosion-prone land and the like. Beautiful part of the country, though. Um, and that there needs to be some way of having bespoke provisions in national direction, and if you want to comment on that as well. Thank you.

Yes, so I'll ask Jocelyne to comment on that. But just to make it c- clear again is that we know we live in an area where we have a special geology and special weather now as well for the last five years. We can't change those. What we can change, though, is what we do with our land and how we use that land. And the work that we have done over the last t- two years, and, and Jocelyne will talk about it, we need more stringent rules in order to protect, our land. And if we don't have those provisions, we, will see the

type of, disasters we've seen in the last five years. So I'll just let Jocelyne tell you a little bit more about what we are asking in order for us to protect our region, the way that it deserves to be protected. Great. Thank you. Um, thank you for the questions.

Um, so as, as you will have read and, and, most likely seen the work around our efforts in, sustainable land use, have culminated across a diverse group of, of efforts, you know, across from community involvement to our, into our policy framework. So we have, applied for, for an extension of our, sustainable land use, plan change.

And one of the, one of the solutions sort of proposed or, or earmarked ultimately is the creation of a sustainable land use plan. And, we, we felt, the freshwater farm plan framework fit really well with the intention and for it to complement our policy framework and our regulatory regime.

Um, so what we would like to see is, alongside the freshwater farm plan framework, the ability for, the council to also, have a sustainable land use plan that further, elaborates on and ultimately goes back to that integrated view, and can speak to and give effect to, managing the highest risk, land that we have in this region. So, and secondly on your, points around Ngā Rohe Moana o Ngāti, nā Hapū o Ngāti Porou and, and the act, in which we've been operating, within since 2019. Um, we definitely

support the provisions across both the bills that we see for not only existing treaty settlements, but also the Takutai Moana legislation, and the, the Ngā Rohe Moana nā, nā Hapū o Ngāti Porou Act 2019. I understand, the importance of our coastal marine environment and, and am enlightened, heartened, we're heartened as a council that these will be recognized and continue to be recognized. Um, the, the desire, through this act is not necessarily for exclusion but i- in fact protection. So,

if, if we can, have the ability to provide for more bespoke regulation, to enable us to look through our nas- natural hazards and our climate change risks, through the standardized, well, the legislation that, that will be accompanying, this rollout through the policy statement or in fact environmental standards we would, we would welcome that.

Uh, well, thank you. F- f- f- unfortunately we have to leave it there. Um, I have got a couple of follow-up questions, but they'll have to wait. Um, so thank you very much. We appreciate your submissions. Um, and we'll, have a great day. Cheers. Thank you. Thank you. Bye-bye. Thank you. Our next submitter, Te Runanga o Ngati Kua.

Kua. I think what they are. Brand. It's only four of them. Kia ora. Morning. Yeah. Very good. Yeah, no problem. Yeah, we can hear you speak up. I think we got you. If you, if you take off the blue, the background there, we'll see both of you.

Uh, kia ora koutou. Ah, there we go. Bingo. Well done. Thank you. Kia ora, good morning, ma. Kia ora koutou nga manuhiri ma. Yeah, yeah. We, we gotcha. Um, yeah, hi.

My name's Grant McCallum. I'm chairing the subcommittee this morning. Uh, we have 15 minutes with you this morning, and we have your submission. Please take it as read, but feel free to highlight a few points and then leave time for questions. Fire away. Thank you. Uh, nga manuhiri, nga reo, nga karangahao ma o te motu, ki hei mauri ora. Uh, tenei te tamo ki a koe e tatiana mana mo te committee me nga whanau ki roto ki te tenei rui.

Uh, mauri ora ki te whare. Uh, ko Tutamapai te maunga. Ko te hoere te awa. Ko te hoere te waka. Raukaaukauata moana. Matua Houtere te tangata. Te Hora te marae. Ngati Kuia te iwi. Mai te iwi karakia. Uh, te iwi pakohi. Uh, my name's Raymond Smith. Um, I am the kaikōrero speaker for Te Rūnanga o Ngāti Kuia today.

I've worked for my iwi for over twenty-five years and hold a bachelor's degree in Kaitiakitanga. Um, Tiakitanga o te whenua, o te awa, o te moana. Uh, treaty negotiator, part of the settlement, PhD development, and, post-settlement imple- implementation, cultural revitalization, and part of the rōpū taiao, that developed the iwi management plan.

Uh, my colleague here to my right is, Eugene Whakahuihui, te Tumu Toi Hau, the CEO of the Te Rūnanga o Ngāti Kuia. Uh, thank you for the opportunity to, to elaborate on our submission today, as, as I see the submission is taken as read. Thank you for that. Uh, ko wai au? Uh, who, who are we? Um, my iwi Ngāti Kuia are descendants of the first people of Te Tau Ihu.

People of Māui, Kupe, Matua Hautere. We have ahikā roa throughout Te Kupe Nga Kuia, top of the South Island, and carry the ancestral responsibility and obligations to uphold, to hol- uphold the mauri of our whenua, our awa, our moana, our hau, our mātauranga tikanga kawa.

Me ngā tāngata me taonga. Uh, our submission has raised ninety-one points and is, and is grounded in Ngā Kanohi Kārearea, the Ngāti Kuia Iwi Environmental Management Plan, and in the commitments/promises made to Ngāti Kuia during negot- na- during the negotiations of our treaty settlement, Te Whakatau.

Um, that is the name of our settlement. Uh, we support it-- we support the submissions of the Te Tau Ihu Collective, a- and, and to Hokainuana, who, was established as our settlement receiving entity to deliver, treaty settlements and provide specialist settlement oversight, who are also ad- adversely affected by this, what we are calling a heinous piece of legislation.

Um, our core issues. The bill does not uphold Te Tiriti. Our central message today is this, the Natural Environment Bill and the Planning Bill are insulting, undermining, dangerous, and do not uphold the intent of Te Tiriti o Waitangi. Agreeing to Te Whakatau, that's, that's Ngāti Kuia Treaty settlement, allowed our people to move out of grievance mode and be involved in mana enhancing future for our kaumatua, pakeke, rangatahi, mokopuna ki roto ki te, ki te ao hurihuri, this changing world. Ngāti Kuia learn from the past, we live for the

future. Uh, Toitū te Tiriti, the treaty negotiations process was a very, very arduous task, working with the likes of Michael Cullen, Parekura Horomia, and all of their advisors in the Labour government, and then the National-led government with John Key, the Ag Party, Māori Party, and the, and the minister Chris Finlayson as, as a negotiator to our settlement Te Whakatau. We were the first ones to go through this treaty settlement based on the principles of the treaty in the, in the top of the South Island, which provided

New Zealand with a whole island settlement. Still hasn't been done anywhere else. Uh, to achieve our enduring relationship at two cents in the dollar and a token land offers of ref- RFRs, first right of refusals, deferred selection, titles with. You know? Ngāti Kuia gave up eighty percent of fisheries guaranteed under the treaty, eighty percent of our cultural space, and it's still not enough. Still attacking our rights and principles under the Tiriti. The treaty is the glue that binds this country and our future.

Tui, tui, tui mātuia. That was the call from Tuia two fifty, two thousand and nineteen when Jenny Shipley, previous leader of the National Party, and Hutariwa Barclay-Kerr led a dual heritage and a shared future kaupapa in this country. The ethos was built into-- from Te Tiriti o Waitangi. E waka eke noa. We're all in this together. The treaty clauses in both bills are narrow, procedural, and omit key treaty principles.

This is a significant backwards step from Section eight of the RMA which required all decision-makers to take treaty principles into account. You know, Section seven, to have regard for Kaitiakitanga. Section six, the recognition and provision of taonga. Right? These are extremely, really important, along with Section five, providing cultural wellbeing, which provides a cultural bottom line process. Uh, the expert advisory group recommended retaining that approach. This government's cabinet explicitly rejected it. Doesn't sound right.

The system cannot function fairly unless all decision-makers are required to act consistently with Te Tiriti. The bills undermine our treaty settlement, Te Whakatau, the Ngāti Kuia Treaty settlement, which was agreed on the understanding that the Crown would uphold our tino rangatiratanga, mana motuhake, our rights, interests, and expectations. The Department of Conservation needs to remain a critical partner under the protocols. Agreed.

Agreed. You know? Our pākui, our mahinga kai, our tīti. All these things are so important about the understanding of the past so we can live for the future. The department-- Um, the, the taonga and species, wāhi tapu, cultural landscapes would continue to protect-- to be protected forever in a manner appropriate and in line with wawata o Ngāti Kuia.

However, the bill cut across these commitments in several ways. Uh, most urgently, the wildlife permitting is shifting away, shifting away from the Wildlife Act. Clause one two eight out of the Natural Environments Bill allows wildlife approvals to be, to be issued as part of the natural resource permit, bypassing the Wildlife Act, and most importantly, Section four. You know? The requirement to give effect to Tiriti.

Very important. This, this means decisions affecting taonga species can be made without the obligation to uphold Tiriti principles or without Department of Conservation, despite DOC's explicit partnership role in our treaty settlement, Te Whakatau. This undermines not just process, but outcomes the settlement was designed to protect.

Um, Māori participation is reduced, not strengthened.That is not the expectation that we were expecting. The bill promises upfront the Māori participation, but it, but it fails to deliver. There is no requirement to, to engage with iwi before notifying the first national policy direction. Iwi are given extremely short timeframes determined at the minister's discretion. This continues to put Ngāti Kuia and financial and capacity issues at the fore.

Notification thresholds reduce iwi ability to exercise kaitiakitanga at the consenting stage. Spatial planning committees and hearing panels are not required to include iwi members. Alienation of key roles for Ngāti Kuia and others in the back seat watching from the sideline again.

It reminds me during the treaty process of the early colonials and the rec- and, and, and the records of keeping a fatherly eye on the natives, and we know what's best for them. The paternalistic treatment of Tangata Whenua is unacceptable. For iwi like Ngāti Kuia, small in size but deeply invested in local relationships, this shifts, this shifts the centralized direction severely limits our ability to influence decisions in our rohe.

Now, the limits in mātauranga are systems gap. Cultural, now, the, the Ngāti Kuia supports environmental limits, environmental limits, but they must include cultural and Māori based limits. The priority of biodiversity values are essential. Mātauranga Māori and how they are set, and ours are set in whakapapa. Companion targets for restoration, not just bottom lines. Without these limits becoming, without these limits-- without these, limits become a tool for managing decline rather than restoring the health of our natural

environment. Um, within our with, w-within our process, NERO, the net enduring restorative outcomes. Its net enduring, enduring restorative outcomes is critical for long-term sustainability. We can't just keep using and degrading our, our natural environment.

What works for iwi? Now, now, with iwi-led approaches, and delivery, we wanna emphasize a strong treaty. Consistent partnership produces excellent outcomes. Ngāti Kuia has demonstrated this through, through our treaty settlements. You know, the Blenheim Airport. You know, prop- property investments right throughout the top of the South Island and, and, and, and, and, and, and investment in infrastructure.

Kotahitanga mō te taiao, the collaborative integrated large scale approach to, to environmental management and long-term environmental management. The Te Huia Eri restoration produc- project, you know, that come from the Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment, you know, the Ministry for the Environment that highlighted sedimentation, you know, and we're still working on stage two.

Whenua ki tai ki moana. You know, from the mountains to the coast, right out to the ocean, wherever these adverse effects are being felt by our native biodiversity and by our people. The Ruapaka, the Ruapaka wetland, you know, very important. I've got a-- one thing, you know, working with industry to create the first finfish open fin- open ocean aquaculture project, really important. Board of Inquiry, RMA, all of those key political things. Um, in closing, you know, for timely, you know, so we can get some questions.

In closing, our request of the committee, Ngāti Kuia expect you to ensure that the apology and promise from the government and agents be upheld. A substantive treaty clause needs to be applied to all decision makers. Treaty settlement and commitments, including DOC's role, are upheld, enabled, and the protocols and promises-- enabling the protocols and promises.

Māori participation is strengthened, not reduced. Input at all levels. Identified Māori land is defined broadly and treated consistency. Sites of significance of wāhi tapu protections are iwi-led and realistic. Limits, including cultural and mātauranga-based measures, long-term and binding.

And a cautionary approach is, is, is necessary, in, in our, in our views. Ngāti Kun- Ngāti Kuia continues to work in partnership to build systems that protects the taiao, our natural environment, and honors the agreements made in good faith. Toitū te tangata, toitū te whenua, toitū te moana, ā, toitū te Tiriti. Uh, we are happy, we're happy to take questions. Uh, Kia ora, thank you very much. Uh, our first question to Rachel Brooking.

Thank you. Uh, in your submission, you talk about the treaty clause, and you want something like section eight back in, but you make a comment about, well, if you are gonna have a, a treaty clause that sets out responsibilities, then it needs to relate to the area. And I'm wondering, for your rohe, what would, what would such a treaty clause look like, and would it need to be specific to each different iwi's rohe?

In, in short, we think that you need to start with having a, a clause which has the same impact. It would say suggest enhancing what's currently in the RMA and obviously pulled into this legislation. What we think is the cumulative effect is that the-- basically it's diminishing, in general what the-- these two pro-- bills propose.

Um, we, we do think, the fact that it is being driven centrally, that there needs to be space for some of those treaty, arrangements and discussions to be enabled at the rohe level. So I think you can still start with something which general-- which cuts across both bills, which provides space for further discussions, relevant to the spaces that are important to us in all iwi, I'd, I'd, I'd suggest.

Thank you. Uh, Ryan Hamilton Tena koutou. I do have a genuine question, and thank you for your submission. There's no uncertainty with what your feelings are around certain issues. Um, this is something which comes up and you'll, you'll be no strangers to the fact it does get a bit of criticism. In- incorporating mātauranga or Māori into some of the...

You talk around settlement, sorry, the, environmental limits, ex- et cetera. Can you give us an example of how that interface would work? The, the Māori model? Yeah, yeah. Sure. Um, part, part of our, you know, understanding right now and, and using the, the, the open ocean, finfish farm that we're doing now, we've, not only doing, done baseline monitoring on there, but our Māori monitoring is about tracking what's already there, tracking change, our quarterly monitoring based on seasonal, issues, seeing, what, what would bring along any adverse

effects from not only industry but environmental, issues as well. Um, just hearing from the, the, the Gisborne District Council or the Dist- District Regional Council, you know, when we had our two storm events down here, and then straight away the cyclone happened up in the East Coast, down, down there. We, we've seen heaps of environmental, issues going, going into the ocean, into the receiving environment.

And so how, how do you- I suppose just to add, to add to that, sorry, is the, you've, you've still got to create the space for us to, to have that, to have that in place. For example, we're well practiced in, applying our Māori model, to doing assessments, across different spa- spaces with different partners.

Um, that is because we've come to the table, we've worked out ways of bringing in their, their science, our mātauranga, and our understanding, not only the way it looks now, but the way it looked when we look through our whakapapa and the way that we hope that it'll grow into over time. So it, it's, it's about enabling the space to have that kōrero so that we can continue to bring in both our perspectives, our understanding of mātauranga, and to inform the establishment of, national direction and, limit setting as well. So again, it's a bit

vague on how we're gonna get to that at the moment across these two bills. So what w- I'd expect from all you is a bit more clarity on how this comes together, and then making sure that we have the opportunity to inject such, such as our Māori model in through limit setting and into the discussions around, national direction as well. Kia ora.

Appreciate that. Thank you. Look, unfortunately we've run out of time. We've, come to the end of your submission period, but thank you very much for your submission today. Very... A lot of passion, a lot of, ent- there, which is great, so have a great day. Thank you. Cheers. Kia ora chair. This committee.

Right. We'll bring up our next submitter. Is the Marlborough, Marlborough District Council. The mayor, I see the mayor there. Ah. Tena koutou, and thank you, Mr. Chair, and thank you for the opportunity, to address the committee today on the Planning Bill and the Natural Environment Bill. You have 15 minutes of your ti- of your, your time. Thank you very much.

Appreciate it, and, look forward to possibly some type of questions once you've addressed this. Really appreciate it. Thank you. Fire away. Thank you. Will do. Uh, look, we recognize these bills will have a significant impact on councils, h- how we function, and how communities and resource users and our industries will interact with the planning system.

Uh, some background to assist the committee. Uh, the Marlborough District Council has operated as a unitary authority for over 30 years. Of course, this means that we have the functions and responsibilities of both a regional and a territorial authority. In terms of the scale of our operation here in Marlborough, we're responsible for a land area of over one million hectares.

That's the largest geographical area of the five unitary councils, excluding the anomaly, which is Auckland. Uh, we have one of the longest and most intricate coastlines in New Zealand, and including the vast Marlborough Sounds. We have a relatively small population here, approximately 52,000 people, but our region consistently performs at a high level with a strong primary production and export sector, and a strong tourism economy.

We are well known, and, and many of you will know us as the largest wine region in the country. Uh, we account for around three quarters of the country's wine production, 70% of New Zealand's vineyard area, and nearly 90% of New Zealand's wine exports. Viticulture underpins our GDP, and I, highlight those facts because they become important, later on, as we get to our request.
